data.title: Docebo boosts full-year revenue guidance on AI platform demand
data.description:
Docebo (DCBO) on Tuesday lifted its full-year revenue outlook, citing demand for its AI workforce training platform.

The company now sees revenue between $271M and $273M, up from prior guidance of $267.5M to $269.5M. Subscription revenue is now forecast at $253.5M to $255.5M, compared with previous guidance of $251.5M to $253.5M.

Analysts expect full-year revenue of $268.60M. Full-year adjusted EBITDA is seen between $54.5M and $56.5M, compared to the prior estimate of $52.5M to $54.5M.

The company said preliminary unaudited revenue for the quarter ended Mar. 31 is expected to be between $65.4M and $65.6M, up 14.3% from $57.3M a year earlier. Analysts are expecting it to be about $63.6M.

Docebo's largest OEM customer is expected to account for 3.2% of annual recurring revenue, down from 9.4% a year earlier. ARR, which is a key software metric, is expected to reach $248.9M as of March 31, up 10.6% from a year earlier. Docebo said foreign exchange movements reduced ARR by $1.4M during the quarter.

“Docebo delivered an exceptional start to 2026, exceeding our expectations across the board and reinforcing the strength of our market positioning. Our performance reflects both disciplined execution and growing demand for our AI workforce readiness platform as organizations prioritize workforce readiness in an increasingly complex operating environment,” said CEO Alessio Artuffo.

The company will report full first-quarter results before markets open on May 8, 2026.

Shares are up around +2.6% in premarket trading on Tuesday.

data.title: Synchrony Q1 earnings beat as credit loss provision declines; unveils dividend boost for Q3
data.description:
Synchrony Financial (SYF) Q1 earnings exceeded the Wall Street consensus on Tuesday as its lower provision for credit losses offset a Q/Q decline in net interest income. It also announced a $6.5B stock repurchase authorization, and the board approved a planned 13% dividend increase, to $0.34 per share, beginning in Q3 2026.

The credit card lender reaffirmed its full-year guidance for EPS at $9.10-$9.50 (midpoint $9.30), compared with the average analyst estimate of $9.21, and ending loan receivables growth in the mid-single digits. It now expects a full-year net charge-off rate of less than 5.5%, compared with its prior outlook of ~5.5%-6.0%.

Q1 GAAP EPS of $2.27, beating the average analyst estimate of $2.20, rose from $2.04 in Q4 2025 and $1.89 in last year’s Q1.

"The combination of higher interest and fees, lower interest expense, and lower net charge-offs supported stronger program performance in the first quarter versus the prior year, which was shared through higher RSA (retailer share arrangements)," said Executive Vice President and Chief Financial Officer Brian Wenzel.

Purchase volume was $43.0B, exceeding the Visible Alpha estimate of $42.5B, falling from $49.5B in Q4, and increasing from $40.7B in Q1 2025.

Net interest income fell to $4.64B from $4.76B in the previous quarter and increased from $4.46B in the year-ago period. The Q1 2026 number lagged the Visible Alpha estimate of $4.69B.

Synchrony (SYF) stock slipped 0.8% in Tuesday premarket trading.

Provision for credit losses of $1.34B, vs. the Visible Alpha consensus of $1.46B, dropped from $1.44B in Q4 and $1.49B in Q1 2025.

Synchrony's (SYF) net charge-offs as a percentage of total average loan receivables were 5.42%, up from 5.37% in the prior quarter and down from 6.38% a year ago.

The 30+ days past due delinquency rate was 4.54% in Q1, compared with 4.49% in Q4 and 4.52% in last year’s Q1.

Loan receivables of $100.1B dipped from $103.8B in the previous quarter.

Conference call at 8:00 AM ET.

data.title: Denarius Metals lifts Emerita offer in renewed acquisition push
Constellium secures multi-year aluminum supply agreement with Airbus
data.description:
Constellium (CSTM) up 3.1% pre-market Tuesday after saying it entered a multi-year agreement with Airbus (EADSF) (EADSY) for the supply of aluminum alloy extrusions, reinforcing its role as a long-term supplier of advanced aluminum solutions for Airbus programs.

Constellium (CSTM) will supply Airbus (EADSF) (EADSY) with a wide range of extrusions manufactured using advanced aluminum alloys; the scope includes bars, and a range of small and large extrusions engineered to meet stringent quality requirements to support demanding aircraft structural applications.

The company said it will supply Airbus (EADSF) (EADSY) from its facilities in Issoire and Montreuil-Juigné, France.

"This agreement reflects Airbus’ trust in our advanced aluminum products and solutions and in our quality performance, industrial reliability, and consistent supply continuity to support long-term aerospace programs," said Philippe Hoffmann, president of Constellium's (CSTM) Aerospace and Transportation unit.

data.title: Earnings Snapshot: 3M beats Q1 earnings estimates; reaffirms FY26 outlook
RTX in charts: Pratt & Whitney Q1 sales rise 11% to $8.17B; backlog reaches $271B
3M earnings beat estimates, stock slips on cautious macro backdrop
data.description:
3M (MMM) reported first-quarter results that matched Wall Street revenue expectations and topped profit forecasts, though shares slipped 1.8% in premarket trading Tuesday as investors weighed a cautious macro backdrop against improving margins and reaffirmed full-year guidance.

The industrial conglomerate posted revenue of $6.0 billion, in line with analysts’ consensus estimate. Adjusted earnings came in at $2.14 a share, ahead of expectations for $1.98.

The results suggest 3M (MMM) is making progress on operational execution and cost discipline even as demand remains uneven across industrial markets. Investors are also watching whether management can sustain margin gains while navigating litigation overhangs, portfolio changes and a volatile global economy.

Earnings fall on GAAP basis

Net income fell to $670 million, or $1.23 a share, from $1.12 billion, or $2.04 a share.

3M (MMM) said GAAP operating margin improved to 23.2% from 20.9%, while adjusted operating margin rose to 23.8% from 23.5%. Adjusted earnings per share increased 14% year over year.

Turnaround efforts continue

“We are executing on 3M's value creation framework to build a stronger company,” Chairman and Chief Executive William Brown said in the earnings release.

Brown added that the company had “a good start to the year” and remains confident in meeting its 2026 outlook despite operating in a volatile environment.

Cash returns remain strong

3M (MMM) returned $2.4 billion to shareholders during the quarter through dividends and share repurchases. Cash from operations totaled $0.6 billion, while adjusted free cash flow was $0.5 billion.

Full-year outlook reaffirmed

3M (MMM) reiterated its full-year 2026 guidance, calling for adjusted total sales growth of about 4%, adjusted operating margin expansion of 70 to 80 basis points and adjusted earnings of $8.50 to $8.70 a share.

The company also expects adjusted operating cash flow of $5.6 billion to $5.8 billion, supporting adjusted free cash flow conversion above 100%.

data.title: Apple's Tim Cook leaving as CEO may be seen as 'mixed,' change in 'strategy' by investors: analysts
data.description:
Apple's (AAPL) Tim Cook stepping down as CEO and hardware chief John Ternus succeeding him at the helm saw strong reactions from analysts. 

SA Analysts

"Tim Cook brought to Apple exactly what was expected of him - operational excellence (as a former COO under Jobs), although little innovation. In my latest Apple breakdown, I said the company’s miss on AI wasn’t a genius master plan, but the simple inability to innovate and achieve tangible results with Apple Intelligence. I assume it was hard for the board to accept the reality of Apple falling behind in the AI race, so the choice of the new CEO seems perfect for the goal of continuing innovation and growing excellence," said Seeking Alpha analyst Julia Ostian.

Cook, who has been CEO for 15 years, will step down from the role and become executive chairman, effective Sept. 1. On Ternus' appointment, Cook said that “John Ternus has the mind of an engineer, the soul of an innovator, and the heart to lead with integrity and with honor. He is a visionary whose contributions to Apple over 25 years are already too numerous to count, and he is without question the right person to lead Apple into the future."

Ostian noted that Cook's quote about Ternus shows that the focus is shifting to innovation and future vision. "I assume Apple will be jumping into the AI race, possibly with some new ideas and ways to implement the Agentic AI, or even create their own solutions. At least, this is what I believe the board expects from the new CEO," Ostian added.

Seeking Alpha analyst Julian Lin, Investing Group Leader for Best Of Breed Growth Stocks, said that "I expect investors to view this change at the top as a possibility of (but not guarantee) a change in the typical “don’t rock the boat” strategy of the predecessor. While AAPL has performed phenomenally well in exercising the “second mover advantage,” its delays in pursuing AI might not lead to the same results. Time will tell if the incoming CEO may seek to change the narrative."

Wall Street Analysts

Wedbush said Tim Cook leaving as CEO in a "shocker headline," while maintaining its Outperform rating and $350 price target on the stock.

"Investors will view this as mixed as this was a sudden move to Executive Chairman as there was clearly a push for change at the C-Suite. These will be big shoes to fill and the timing of Cook exiting stage left as CEO could make sense but also creates questions. Apple is making a major transition on its AI strategy and longtime CEO and legendary Cook leaving now is a surprise. There was growing pressure on Apple to produce a successful AI strategy and Cook must feel that the pieces are now in place heading into WWDC [Worldwide Developers Conference] to hand over the reigns at this time," said analysts led by Dan Ives.

The analysts noted that Cook leaves a lasting legacy in Cupertino, and there will be a lot of pressure on Ternus to produce success out of the gates, especially on the AI front. While there were rumors of Cook leaving as CEO, investors will, for now, have more questions than answers around the timing and what this means for the broader Apple strategy, the analysts added.

"This will put even more pressure on Apple to produce success and its product roadmap at WWDC with AI front and center," said Ives and his team.

Apple also appointed Johny Srouji as chief hardware officer. Srouji, who served as senior vice president of hardware technologies, will now assume an expanded role leading hardware engineering, which Ternus recently oversaw, as well as the hardware technologies organization. Arthur Levinson, who had been the non-executive chairman at Apple for the past 15 years, will become its lead independent director, effective Sept. 1. Ternus will also become a board member, effective Sept. 1.

The analysts said in a separate note that there are many opportunities for Ternus to define his own path and the future success of Apple.

Ives and his team believe there are three keys for Ternus: AI; hardware and innovation; and keeping the Cupertino culture but bringing in more outsiders and mergers and acquisitions, or M&A.

The analysts noted that the reputation of Ternus is very strong both internally in Cupertino and externally in the tech industry.

"In this AI World Apple has more cash, consumers, and brand recognition than any company in the world...but now is the time to flex the muscles and go on the offensive instead of the defensive. Ternus is not going to take over the CEO baton with a treadmill approach in our view and this is a good thing. Apple needs to balance keeping all the great culture with some new innovation going forward as the foundation is there in Apple Park...but Meta, OpenAI, Google, Anthropic, Samsung, Huawei...and countless others are going for market share in this new AI era," said Ives and his team.

J.P. Morgan kept its Overweight rating on the tech giant's shares.

"We view the announcement positively with the elevation of a product/hardware focussed executive to the top role, particularly given the backdrop of intense market competition to deliver next generation form factors within smartphones and beyond smartphones that can potentially be the medium of widescale AI consumption, while also benefiting from Cook’s leadership with policymakers globally," said analysts led by Samik Chatterjee.

The analysts noted that key areas on the top of the mind for investors with this transition, although not a complete surprise, would be: Firstly, continuation of the best-in-class execution that has been a hallmark of Cook's leadership with the transition to new leadership.

Secondly, the analysts said that the position of Services in the overall order of priorities from new leadership with a background focused on hardware innovation. Lastly, the analysts noted that, updates and potential changes in AI strategy, where investors are also awaiting the launch of a more personalized Siri, potentially going into WWDC later this year.

Morgan Stanley maintained its Overweight rating on the stock.

Analysts led by Erik Woodring said that Apple's CEO change is unlikely to alter the company's core strategy/vision across hardware, services, software, capital returns, or vertical integration. However, the analysts added that with a CEO transition, they can see some renewed investor optimism. While near-term changes are unlikely, the potential for long-term change increases, according to the analysts.

Woodring and his team noted that Apple's press release somewhat lays clues for the path forward. Firstly, the analysts said that Tim Cook would not step down for another four months, ensuring a seamless/telegraphed CEO transition and enabling him to remain at the helm for a very critical June WWDC, but then allow Ternus to lead the September iPhone launch event.

Secondly, the analysts said that  Ternus has been an important part of Apple product launches for over two decades, and promoting him to CEO clearly shows Apple's emphasis on product at the center of the flywheel will remain. Thirdly, the analysts noted that Cook remaining executive chairman and "engaging with policymakers around the world" shows Cook will remain a critical conduit between Apple and political leaders around the world, a role Cook has excelled at.

Lastly, Woodring and his team said that Srouji assuming a broader role within Hardware engineering likely means even greater collaboration between the hardware and silicon teams (i.e., more custom silicon) going forward.

Shares of Apple dipped about 0.3% premarket on Tuesday.

Update: The story was updated with J.P. Morgan, Morgan Stanley, and Wedbush analysts' notes.

data.title: Danaher rises after lifting full-year earnings outlook
data.description:
Danaher (DHR) shares traded higher in the premarket on Tuesday after the life sciences company lifted its full-year earnings outlook at the midpoint following a mixed performance with its Q1 2026 results.

The Washington, DC-based healthcare giant reaffirmed its growth outlook for non-GAAP core revenue at a 3% YoY to 6% YoY range, in line with the ~4.3% YoY in the consensus.

However, the company revised its non-GAAP diluted net earnings per share outlook to $8.35–8.55, indicating $8.45 at the midpoint compared to $8.40 in the consensus and $8.43 in the prior forecast.

Its Q1 revenue figure of $5.95B missed the forecast by $50M despite ~4% YoY growth, as its Diagnostics segment, which houses its Cepheid molecular diagnostics unit, contracted ~2% YoY, generating $2.42B, falling short of $2.49B in the consensus, per Bloomberg data.

However, the company’s Biotechnology and Life Sciences segments grew ~12% YoY and ~4% YoY, respectively, adding $1.74B and $1.80B to the topline, exceeding $1.73B and $1.79B in the consensus, respectively.

“On the top line, we continued on a steady recovery path with strength in Bioprocessing and better-than-expected performance in Life Sciences largely offsetting the impact of a lighter-than-typical Q1 respiratory season at Cepheid,” CEO Rainer Blair remarked.

As for the bottom line, DHR’s non-GAAP earnings per share grew ~10% YoY to $2.06, beating Street forecasts by $0.12 as its adjusted operating margin improved 60 bps from the prior year period to 30.2%.

Looking ahead to Q2, the company projects its non-GAAP core revenue to grow in the low-single-digit percent range from the prior-year period, compared to ~3.2% YoY growth in the consensus.

data.title: Valmont beats top-line and bottom-line estimates; raises FY26 outlook
data.description:
